title: Deformation mechanisms in nanotwinned metal nanopillars
abstract: Nanotwinned metals are attractive in many applications because they simultaneously demonstrate high strength and high ductility, characteristics that are usually thought to be mutually exclusive. However, most nanotwinned metals are produced in polycrystalline forms and therefore contain randomly oriented twin and grain boundaries making it difficult to determine the origins of their useful mechanical properties. Here, we report the fabrication of arrays of vertically aligned copper nanopillars that contain a very high density of periodic twin boundaries and no grain boundaries or other microstructural features. We use tension experiments, transmission electron microscopy and atomistic simulations to investigate the influence of diameter, twin-boundary spacing and twin-boundary orientation on the mechanical responses of individual nanopillars. We observe a brittle-to-ductile transition in samples with orthogonally oriented twin boundaries as the twin-boundary spacing decreases below a critical value (∼ 3-4 nm for copper). We also find that nanopillars with slanted twin boundaries deform via shear offsets and significant detwinning. The ability to decouple nanotwins from other microstructural features should lead to an improved understanding of the mechanical properties of nanotwinned metals.
results: Experiments were performed on samples with four different internal/external geometries with the specific characteristic length scales as listed in Table 1. Figure 2 shows tensile engineering stress-strain curves for samples D50A0 (Fig. 2a), D50A18 (Fig. 2b), D70A0 (Fig. 2c), D100A0 (Fig. 2d) and D250A0 (Fig. 2e), with insets showing post-deformation SEM images (Fig. 2b,d,e) and dark-field (Fig. 2c) and bright-field (Fig. 2a) TEM images. The letters D and A in the specimen names indicate sample diameter and twin-boundary tilt angle with respect to the pillar axis. Intriguingly, the 100-nm-diameter pillars with orthogonal twin boundaries spaced at 4.3 nm (Fig. 2d) appear to exhibit the characteristics of brittle fracture, that is, linear elastic loading followed by sudden failure without noticeable plasticity. The tensile stresses at fracture range from 1.81 to 2.45 GPa, with an average of 2.12 GPa, a value on the order of ∼ 40% of the ideal strength of copper (ref. 19) and 1.5 times higher than the ultimate tensile strength of single-crystalline copper nanopillars with similar diameters tested using an identical methodology. The post-deformation SEM image presented in the inset of Fig. 2d reveals no evidence of necking or shape change, suggesting that the failure is of a brittle nature. This is further supported by the fracture surface being nearly perpendicular to the loading axis, rather than slanted at 45°. In contrast, the 250-nm-diameter samples with orthogonal twin boundaries spaced at 2.8 nm (Fig. 2e), the 70-nm-diameter samples with orthogonal twin boundaries spaced at 0.6 nm (Fig. 2c) and the 50-nm-diameter samples with orthogonal and slanted twin boundaries spaced at 1.2 nm (Fig. 2a,b) show enhanced plasticity. The engineering stress–strain curves in Fig. 2a–c,e show extended plastic regimes without any appreciable work-hardening. The insets in Fig. 2a–c,e show clear neck formation, further supporting the observation of localized plasticity in these nanopillars.
discussion: Atomistic simulations (Supplementary Discussion S2) revealed that twin planes can serve as cleavage planes during brittle fracture. However, we found that when the twin boundaries in nanotwinned copper were spaced sufficiently close to one another, the high stresses at the crack tip induced twinning dislocation nucleation and propagation on twin boundaries in the vicinity of the crack tip. This led to a cascade of subsequent dislocation activities and eventually to ductile failure via plastic deformation (Supplementary Movies S1-S3). This observation is fully corroborated by our experimental results, in which the nanopillars with twin-boundary spacing at or below 2.8 nm exhibited clear characteristics of ductile fracture, including extended plasticity and shear localization via necking. In contrast, nanopillars with a twin-boundary spacing of 4.3 nm deformed in a brittle fashion associated with the intrinsic brittleness of twin boundaries. These distinct deformation mechanisms revealed by the atomistic simulations indicate that there exists a twin-spacing-induced transition between intrinsically brittle and intrinsically ductile behaviours in nanotwinned metals, which is consistent with our experimental observations. More detailed information on this brittle/ductile fracture is provided in Supplementary Discussion S2.
conclusion: In summary, we report the fabrication of free-standing copper nanopillars with densely spaced twin boundaries in the absence of grain boundaries and initial dislocations. The orientation of nanotwin lamellae can be controlled so that the twin boundaries are either perpendicular or angled at 188 with respect to the pillar axis. We find that all nanotwinned nanopillars with twin spacings of 2.8 nm or below exhibit non-trivial plasticity, with tensile strengths ranging from 0.7 to 2.4 GPa (Table 1). We have shown that the difference in strength can be attributed to their distinct deformation mechanisms: strain localization (necking and shear banding) caused by twin boundary-dislocation interactions dominates plastic deformation in orthogonal twin-boundary samples, whereas detwinning is a governing deformation mechanism in slanted twin-boundary samples. Despite these differences, deformation in both types of structures is enabled by dislocation nucleation at the twin boundary-surface intersections, with their subsequent activity dictated by the shear stress acting along the twin boundaries. Pillars with a diameter of 100 nm and orthogonal twin boundaries demonstrate a ductile-to-brittle transition as the twin boundary spacing decreases, reaching a tensile strength of 2.12 GPa. Such brittle fracture might be associated with the intrinsic brittleness of twin boundaries, as supported by atomistic simulations. The present work discerns the specific role of twin boundaries in the deformation of small-scale structures and sheds further light on the dislocation-nucleation mechanisms responsible for the high plasticity observed in nanosize structures.
